statusLine: EU fuel stocks sit at watch level in March 2026, with 15 of 27 member states holding at least one fuel type below the mandatory 90-day minimum and jet fuel emerging as the most widespread vulnerability across the bloc.
fullAnalysis:
As of March 2026, EU-wide average stock levels stand at 95.6 days for petrol, 88.1 days for diesel, and 87.0 days for jet fuel — placing both diesel and jet fuel below the mandatory 90-day net import minimum. While the bloc-wide average remains close to the threshold, these figures mask significant variation across member states. The EU average is being supported by a small number of well-stocked countries; the median picture is considerably tighter. Overall system status is assessed at watch.

Jet fuel is the most acute pressure point across the bloc. Of the 27 countries in the dataset, 19 are carrying jet fuel stocks below the 90-day threshold, with several — including Poland (22.5 days), Portugal (21.2 days), and Finland (31.9 days) — holding critically low levels that represent a serious near-term vulnerability for aviation supply chains. This is particularly notable given that several EU member states have recently drawn down strategic reserves in response to Middle East supply disruptions, leaving less buffer capacity available. Diesel stocks are also under pressure: 15 of 27 countries report diesel below the mandatory minimum, with Romania at 30.2 days and Slovakia at 33.5 days representing the most constrained positions.

At the country level, Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, Romania, and Portugal each hold all three fuel types at critical status, indicating broad-based reserve deficits rather than isolated product shortfalls. France, Italy, and Czechia — larger economies with significant consumption — also carry critical ratings across multiple fuel categories, adding systemic weight to the headline numbers. By contrast, Estonia, Ireland, and Latvia report comfortable buffers across all products, and Finland and the Netherlands hold strong diesel positions (197.7 and 207.5 days respectively) despite being critical on jet fuel.

On the price side, the EU average as of 15 June 2026 stands at €1.771 per litre for petrol and €1.781 for diesel. Brent crude has risen 11.2% since late February 2026 — from $72.48 to $80.59 per barrel — reflecting the ongoing impact of geopolitical tension on global crude markets. This price appreciation, combined with reduced strategic reserve buffers in multiple member states, means the bloc has less room to absorb further supply shocks than the EU-wide averages alone would suggest. Policymakers should note that any further escalation in Middle East supply routes or a cold-weather demand spike could rapidly move several currently watch-rated countries into critical territory.
keyPoints: Jet fuel is the most widespread vulnerability: 19 of 27 member states hold jet fuel stocks below the mandatory 90-day minimum, including Portugal (21.2 days) and Poland (22.5 days) at critically low levels.
Both EU-average diesel (88.1 days) and jet fuel (87.0 days) sit below the 90-day mandatory minimum as of March 2026, though EU-average petrol (95.6 days) remains above threshold.

5 of 27 countries — Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, Romania, and Portugal — are rated critical across all three fuel types simultaneously, indicating systemic reserve deficits rather than isolated shortfalls.
Brent crude has risen 11.2% since late February 2026 to $80.59/barrel, and strategic reserve drawdowns in response to Middle East disruptions have reduced the bloc's available buffer capacity.

A small number of member states — notably Estonia, Ireland, Finland (diesel), and the Netherlands (diesel) — maintain strong reserves that are partially masking the tighter picture in the bloc's median and lower-quartile countries.
